evals.prompt:
Just got off a 40-min discovery call with Lisa Cheng, VP of Operations at Norwood Distribution. Need to send the follow-up ASAP before she goes into her next meeting.

Here's my call notes:
- They're doing 14,000 SKUs across 3 warehouses, picking accuracy is at 94.2% and she said "we're bleeding margin on mis-picks and returns" -- exact words
- Their current WMS is a legacy Oracle system from 2018, she called it "held together with duct tape and prayers"
- Peak season (Oct-Dec) they bring on 120 temp workers and ramp time is 3 weeks -- she said that's "the thing keeping me up at night" because they lost $1.2M in Q4 fulfillment penalties last year
- She was very interested when I showed how our guided picking cuts ramp time to ~4 days. Asked if we had a case study from another 3PL
- Her CFO needs to approve anything over $200K -- she said he's "numbers-driven, show me the ROI and I'll get it done"
- We agreed I'd send the ProLog 3PL case study and a rough ROI model by Thursday, and she'd loop in the CFO for a 30-min call next Tuesday at 2pm ET

Send the post-discovery follow-up.
evals.expected_output: A Type 1 Post-Discovery Follow-Up email sent within 2 hours. Subject line references Norwood Distribution's specific pain (picking accuracy, fulfillment penalties, or peak season ramp time -- not generic 'our call'). 'What I heard' section includes 2-3 bullets using Lisa's exact language ('bleeding margin on mis-picks', 'held together with duct tape and prayers', 'the thing keeping me up at night'). 'What we discussed' references guided picking and ramp time reduction without turning it into a product pitch. Next step confirms: sending ProLog 3PL case study and ROI model by Thursday, and the CFO call scheduled for Tuesday at 2pm ET. Timing recommendation is within 2 hours / immediately. Sign-off is clean, one name.
expectations: Subject line references a specific pain from the call — picking accuracy, fulfillment penalties, or peak season ramp time at Norwood Distribution (drives reply rate) — a subject line that mirrors the prospect's own language gets opened because it signals 'I was listening', not 'here's my template'

'What I heard' section contains at least 2 of Lisa's exact phrases: 'bleeding margin on mis-picks', 'held together with duct tape and prayers', 'the thing keeping me up at night', or '$1.2M in Q4 fulfillment penalties' (drives deal velocity) — mirroring the prospect's own words in the recap builds trust that you understood the problem, which is the #1 factor in whether they show up to the next call

Next step section confirms two specific commitments with dates: (1) ProLog 3PL case study and ROI model by Thursday, and (2) the 30-minute CFO call on Tuesday at 2pm ET (drives deal velocity) — a follow-up without explicit dated next steps lets the deal stall; locking in deliverables + a calendar hold with the economic buyer keeps the deal moving toward close

Email structures content into distinct 'What I heard' and 'What we discussed' sections with the prospect's pain leading before any product mention (drives reply rate) — this separation proves the rep listened before pitching, and gives the prospect something to forward to the CFO that frames the problem in their own language

Email avoids all banned follow-up phrases: 'just checking in', 'circling back', 'bumping this up', 'touching base', and does not open with a generic 'great to meet you' without specifics (drives reply rate) — these phrases signal a template and train buyers to ignore future emails from the rep

Timing recommendation specifies sending within 2 hours of the call (drives deal velocity) — post-discovery follow-ups sent within 2 hours have significantly higher open and reply rates than those sent next-day, because the conversation is still top-of-mind and the prospect hasn't context-switched

evals.prompt:
Finished a demo with Marcus Webb, Director of Engineering at Stratos Payments, and his team (2 senior engineers were on). Need to send the post-demo follow-up.

Demo context:
- Showed them our API gateway platform -- they're replacing a homegrown system that's costing them 40 engineering hours/week in maintenance
- Marcus was visibly impressed by the traffic-splitting feature, asked 3 follow-up questions about canary deployments specifically
- One of his engineers, Priya, raised a hard objection: she's worried about vendor lock-in because their current architecture uses custom middleware and she asked "what happens if we need to migrate off your platform in 2 years?" I showed her the open-standards export and she seemed partially satisfied but said she'd need to see the schema documentation
- Marcus said he wants to move fast -- his CTO gave him a Q2 deadline to have the new gateway live
- We agreed I'd send a proposal by end of next week (March 20) and we'd do a proposal review call on March 24 at 10am PT
- Marcus asked if we could do a 60-day pilot instead of full commitment -- I said I'd include pilot terms in the proposal

Send the post-demo follow-up.
evals.expected_output: A Type 2 Post-Demo Follow-Up email. Subject line follows the '[Company] + [Your Company] -- next steps' pattern or references a specific demo topic. Body acknowledges Marcus's reaction to traffic-splitting / canary deployments specifically. Addresses Priya's vendor lock-in objection directly (references open-standards export, commits to sending schema documentation). Confirms proposal delivery by March 20 with 60-day pilot terms included. Confirms proposal review call on March 24 at 10am PT. References the Q2 deadline as a shared urgency signal. Timing recommendation present.
expectations: Body specifically names Marcus's positive reaction to the traffic-splitting feature or canary deployment capability — not a generic 'the team seemed interested in the demo' (drives deal velocity) — calling out the exact feature that sparked engagement reinforces the 'aha moment' and gives Marcus language to champion internally when he sells this to his CTO

Priya's vendor lock-in objection is addressed directly: references the open-standards export shown during the demo and commits to sending the schema documentation she requested (drives deal velocity) — unaddressed technical objections from influencers are the #1 reason post-demo deals stall; resolving Priya's concern in writing prevents her from becoming a silent blocker

Proposal timeline confirms two specific dates: proposal delivery by March 20 with 60-day pilot terms included, and proposal review call on March 24 at 10am PT (drives deal velocity) — documenting agreed dates in the follow-up creates mutual accountability and prevents the 'I never agreed to that timeline' objection that kills Q2 deadlines

Email references the Q2 deadline or CTO-imposed timeline as shared urgency (drives deal velocity) — anchoring to the buyer's own deadline reframes urgency as their goal, not the seller pushing for a close, which keeps the champion motivated to drive internal approvals

Email avoids all banned follow-up phrases: 'just checking in', 'circling back', 'bumping this up', 'touching base', and does not use generic openers without specific demo content (drives reply rate) — these phrases signal a mass template and undermine the credibility built during a personalized demo

Timing recommendation specifies sending within 24 hours of the demo (drives deal velocity) — post-demo follow-ups sent within 24 hours lock in momentum while the product experience is still vivid; delays past 24 hours let competing priorities and internal objections fill the gap

evals.prompt:
I need to re-engage a stalled deal. Rachel Kim, Head of Talent Acquisition at Evergrow Staffing, went dark 3 weeks ago. Here's the history:

- Had a great discovery call on Feb 12 -- she was dealing with 6,000 applicants/month and only 2 recruiters, said screening was "drowning us"
- Demo on Feb 19 went well, she said our AI screening "could save us 30 hours a week minimum"
- Sent proposal on Feb 24 with a review call booked for Feb 28
- She canceled the Feb 28 review call morning-of, said "something came up, will reschedule"
- I sent a reschedule link on Feb 28 -- no response
- Sent a check-in on March 4 -- no response
- It's now March 11, haven't heard from her in 11 days since the cancellation

Demo context:

I don't want to lose this deal but I also don't want to be that annoying rep. Write the re-engagement email.
evals.expected_output: A Type 4 Stalled Deal Re-Engagement email. Subject line follows the pattern '[Company] -- checking in honestly' or similar direct, non-salesy framing. Body acknowledges the silence without guilt-tripping (no 'I've tried reaching you several times' tone). Gives Rachel an easy exit ('if priorities have shifted, completely fine -- just let me know and I'll close this out'). Gives an easy re-entry ('if you're still evaluating, happy to pick up where we left off' or similar). Does NOT use banned phrases. Does NOT recap the full sales history or re-pitch the product. Keeps it short -- under 100 words in the body. Timing recommendation present. Next action specifies what to do if no response (e.g., one final follow-up in 7-10 days, then close-lost).
expectations: Email body is under 100 words and does NOT recap the full deal history or re-pitch the product (drives reply rate) — stalled deal re-engagement emails that dump prior meeting notes and product benefits read as pressure; short, low-pressure messages get reopened because they cost the prospect almost nothing to respond to

An easy exit is explicitly offered — something like 'if priorities have shifted, that's completely fine' or 'if now isn't the right time, just let me know and I'll close this out' (drives pipeline hygiene) — giving permission to say no is counterintuitively the highest-converting re-engagement tactic because it removes the guilt that causes ghosting, and it lets the rep clean dead deals out of the pipeline

An easy re-entry is explicitly offered — something like 'if you're still evaluating, happy to pick up where we left off' (drives deal velocity) — this lowers the activation energy for the prospect to re-engage without requiring them to explain the gap or commit to a full sales cycle restart

Email acknowledges the silence respectfully without guilt-tripping — no language like 'I've tried reaching you multiple times' or 'I haven't heard back from my last 2 emails' that tallies up ignored messages (drives reply rate) — counting ignored messages triggers defensiveness and trains the prospect to keep ignoring; respectful acknowledgment preserves the relationship for re-engagement

Email avoids all banned follow-up phrases: 'just checking in', 'circling back', 'bumping this up', 'touching base' (drives reply rate) — these phrases are the single strongest signal to a buyer that an email is a template, and they correlate with immediate deletion

Next action section specifies a concrete plan if Rachel does not respond — e.g., one more follow-up in 7-10 days then mark closed-lost or move to nurture (drives pipeline hygiene) — without a defined exit plan, stalled deals linger in the pipeline indefinitely, inflating forecast and masking real pipeline health from management
